diseases.name: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (Lupus)
diseases.description: A chronic autoimmune disease where the body's immune system attacks its own tissues — skin, joints, kidneys, heart and brain.
diseases.so_what: Nine out of every ten people living with lupus are women.
diseases.citation: Sex- and gender-related differences in systemic lupus erythematosus: a scoping review (PMC, 2024)

diseases.name: Osteoporosis
diseases.description: A silent bone-thinning disease that makes fractures much more likely after age 50 — often discovered only after a broken hip or wrist.
diseases.so_what: Women over 50 carry roughly 4.5x the diagnosed osteoporosis risk of men.
diseases.citation: Sarafrazi N, Wambogo EA, Shepherd JA. Osteoporosis or Low Bone Mass in Older Adults, United States, 2017-2018. CDC NCHS Data Brief No. 405, 2021
diseases.publisher: CDC National Center for Health Statistics

diseases.name: Migraine
diseases.description: More than a bad headache — recurring, often disabling neurological attacks with nausea, light and sound sensitivity that can last for days.
diseases.so_what: Migraine is the leading cause of disability for women under 50 worldwide.
diseases.citation: Buse DC et al. Sex differences in the prevalence, symptoms, and associated features of migraine — AMPP Study. PubMed, 2013
diseases.publisher: PubMed / American Migraine Prevalence and Prevention Study

diseases.name: Alzheimer's Disease
diseases.description: A progressive brain disease that destroys memory and thinking — the most common cause of dementia in older adults.
diseases.so_what: By age 45, a woman's lifetime risk of Alzheimer's is 1 in 5 — twice a man's.
diseases.citation: Alzheimer's disease as a women's health challenge (PMC, 2024)

diseases.name: Major Depression
diseases.description: A persistent mood disorder that affects how a person feels, thinks and handles daily life — far more than just sadness.
diseases.so_what: Twice as many women as men live with depression — in nearly every country measured.
diseases.citation: Albert PR. Why is depression more prevalent in women? Journal of Psychiatry & Neuroscience. PMC, 2015

hook_anchor.claim: In Zambia's 2017 STEPS survey, women were 24% more likely than men to carry multiple non-communicable disease risk factors — and Zambian sub-studies put female diabetes prevalence at 3.0% vs 2.1% in men.
hook_anchor.citation: Pengpid S, Peltzer K. Prevalence and correlates of multiple non-communicable disease risk factors among adults in Zambia: results of the first national STEPS survey in 2017. PMC, 2021
